_description: Eval set for chunking strategy comparison. Each question has a 'reference' passage that must appear (substring match, case-insensitive, whitespace-tolerant) inside any retrieved chunk for it to count as relevant. All questions are in English (the document language). Cross-lingual retrieval was tested in an earlier iteration and consistently degraded recall by ~15% with monolingual embedders — kept as a known limitation in the README, not in the eval. Source PDF: data/pdfs/2605.11157v1.pdf (Nicholas Teh — The Price of Proportional Representation in Temporal Voting, arXiv:2605.11157v1).

questions.question: How does the paper describe the temporal voting model?
questions.reference: framework for long-term collective decision-making

questions.question: How does the paper formalize the tension between social welfare and proportionality?
questions.reference: worst-case ratio between the maximum achievable utilitarian welfare and the maximum welfare attainable subject to a proportionality axiom

questions.question: What ML-driven system examples does the paper use to motivate repeated exposure allocation?
questions.reference: selecting a featured creator each day, a highlighted job posting each week

questions.question: What is the qualitative bound on welfare loss when enforcing proportional representation?
questions.reference: growing, yet sublinear, welfare loss

questions.question: How is PJR positioned relative to JR and EJR in the axiom hierarchy?
questions.reference: intermediate axiom between JR and EJR

questions.question: What is the asymptotic behavior of the welfare loss under the JR axiom as the time horizon grows?
questions.reference: welfare loss diminishes as the time horizon grows and vanishes asymptotically

questions.question: What does Lemma 4.2 establish about EJR+ under static preferences?
questions.reference: an outcome satisfies EJR+ iff it satisfies EJR

questions.question: What complexity class does the paper place the Phi-UTIL decision problem in?
questions.reference: is NP-complete, even when voters have static preferences

questions.question: How is utilitarian welfare defined in this paper?
questions.reference: the total number of approving voter-round pairs

questions.question: What term does the paper use for groups of voters who react similarly across rounds?
questions.reference: behavioral types

questions.question: What algorithmic technique gives the FPT result for JR-MAXUTIL parameterized by the number of voter types?
questions.reference: we obtain a dynamic program

questions.question: What structural parameter makes welfare maximization tractable via FPT algorithms?
questions.reference: parameterized by the number of voter types

questions.question: What qualitative claim does the paper make about the time horizon and the cost of JR?
questions.reference: increasing the time horizon can make JR cheap

questions.question: What are the two central questions the paper sets out to answer?
questions.reference: How costly is enforcing proportional representation in temporal voting

questions.question: Why does the paper focus on EJR+ rather than FJR?
questions.reference: while FJR is always satisfiable

questions.question: What related model does the paper distinguish itself from where decisions depend on the history of past rounds?
questions.reference: perpetual voting model

questions.question: What kind of proportionality axiom do Elkind et al. (2024b) study, which the paper distinguishes from its own focus?
questions.reference: individual proportionality notion

questions.question: How does the paper characterize the novelty of its contribution?
questions.reference: first systematic study of the efficiency cost

questions.question: What structural restrictions are used in the hardness reductions?
questions.reference: even under static preferences and constant approval-degree

questions.question: What does the paper prove about welfare maximization being approximable?
questions.reference: APX-hard
